prior.summary: MONITORING. Key source for emerging market intelligence. ACTION NOW: Include UNCTAD in monitoring for Latin America, Africa, and Asia-Pacific trade lane developments. Owner: Strategy + Operations.
prior.what_is_it: UNCTAD Sustainable Transport programme tracks freight-specific sustainability developments in developing and emerging economies. Publishes the Review of Maritime Transport (annual), Trade Facilitation in Times of Crisis reports, and port sustainability assessments. Key source for understanding regulatory developments in Global South trade lanes.
prior.why_matters: UNCTAD analysis covers trade lanes that Western-focused sources miss — Africa, Southeast Asia, Latin America, small island developing states. For freight forwarders: (1) emerging market regulations on port emissions, fuel quality, and customs modernization are tracked here, (2) UNCTAD maritime transport review provides shipping rate and capacity forecasts, (3) trade facilitation assessments affect customs processing times on developing-country routes.

prior.full_brief:
# UNCTAD Sustainable Transport: Maritime and Inland Freight Decarbonization Policy Intelligence

---

## 1. Purpose and Scope of This Document

This brief synthesises intelligence from UNCTAD's transport and trade logistics programme, the IMO's 2023 revised GHG strategy (adopted at MEPC 80), the UNECE Inland Transport Committee's (ITC) landmark February 2024 decarbonization strategy, and supporting analysis from the ITF/OECD Decarbonising Transport initiative and the ICCT. It covers the regulatory and policy architecture governing sustainable transport across ocean, road, rail, and inland waterway modes — the modes central to global freight forwarding operations.

The brief is written for workspaces operating as freight forwarders and logistics intermediaries across air-primary, road-secondary, and ocean-tertiary transport, with trade lane exposure across the Americas, Europe, and Asia. Relevant cargo verticals are noted where the policy creates differential exposure.

**Format type:** Market Signal Brief (item_type: initiative)

**Convention note:** All binding-obligation language in cited source material is reproduced verbatim and cited. The workspace's own compliance obligations are assessed under labeled ANALYSIS or LEGAL callouts, never as bare assertion. Dates from source documents are stated as FACTS with inline citation.

---

## 2. What's Moving and What Triggered It

**MONITORING**

Two parallel UN-system decarbonization architectures are now operative simultaneously — one governing international maritime shipping, one governing inland transport (road, rail, inland waterway) — and their 2050 net-zero trajectories are on a convergence course that will reshape freight cost structures, carrier compliance obligations, and shipper disclosure requirements across every mode the workspace uses.

**Maritime track.** Member States of the International Maritime Organization (IMO), meeting at the Marine Environment Protection Committee (MEPC 80), adopted the 2023 IMO Strategy on Reduction of GHG Emissions from Ships on 7 July 2023. *Source: IMO Press Briefing, IMO, 7 July 2023. https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/pressbriefings/pages/revised-ghg-reduction-strategy-for-global-shipping-adopted-.aspx

The revised strategy sets "an enhanced common ambition to reach net-zero GHG emissions from international shipping by or around, i.e. close to, 2050." *Source: IMO Press Briefing, IMO, 7 July 2023. https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/pressbriefings/pages/revised-ghg-reduction-strategy-for-global-shipping-adopted-.aspx

Indicative checkpoints are set at reducing total annual GHG emissions from international shipping "by at least 20%, striving for 30%, by 2030, compared to 2008" and "by at least 70%, striving for 80%, by 2040, compared to 2008." *Source: IMO Press Briefing, IMO, 7 July 2023. https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/pressbriefings/pages/revised-ghg-reduction-strategy-for-global-shipping-adopted-.aspx

The strategy also sets a 2030 fuel-mix target: "uptake of zero or near-zero GHG emission technologies, fuels and/or energy sources to represent at least 5%, striving for 10%, of the energy used by international shipping by 2030." *Source: IMO Press Briefing, IMO, 7 July 2023. https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/pressbriefings/pages/revised-ghg-reduction-strategy-for-global-shipping-adopted-.aspx

**Inland transport track.** UN member States adopted the UNECE Inland Transport Committee's (ITC) Strategy on Reducing Greenhouse Gas Emissions from Inland Transport on 23 February 2024. *Source: UNECE Press Release, UNECE, 26 February 2024. https://unece.org/media/press/388574

The ITC strategy's main objective is "for ITC to take urgent action to assist its member States and Contracting Parties to United Nations legal instruments under its purview in achieving the aspirational goal of net zero GHG emissions from inland transport by 2050." *Source: UNECE Press Release, UNECE, 26 February 2024. https://unece.org/media/press/388574

**UNCTAD programme framing.** UNCTAD describes sustainable transport as "a pre-condition for shared prosperity" and operates programmes including climate-smart trade facilitation in Africa and Asia-Pacific, sustainable and resilient food supply chains, and sustainable smart ports for African countries. *Source: UNCTAD Transport, Logistics and Trade Facilitation topic page, UNCTAD, accessed 2026. https://unctad.org/topic/transport-and-trade-logistics

*Analytical inference:* The simultaneity of these two strategies — maritime and inland, both targeting 2050 net zero — means that freight forwarders coordinating multimodal shipments will face compounding regulatory exposure: ocean carriers under IMO instruments, road and rail carriers under evolving national implementations of the ITC framework, and potentially both on a single shipment moving via sea-road or sea-rail combinations.

---

## 3. Who's Driving It and What They Want

**IMO (maritime):** The IMO is "the United Nations specialized agency with responsibility for developing global standards for shipping and supporting countries to implement those rules." *Source: IMO Press Briefing, IMO, 7 July 2023. https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/pressbriefings/pages/revised-ghg-reduction-strategy-for-global-shipping-adopted-.aspx The 2023 strategy was adopted with "unanimous support from all Member States." *Source: IMO Press Briefing, IMO, 7 July 2023. https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/pressbriefings/pages/revised-ghg-reduction-strategy-for-global-shipping-adopted-.aspx IMO's stated intent is to develop a "basket of candidate measures" combining "a technical element, namely a goal-based marine fuel standard regulating the phased reduction of the marine fuel's GHG intensity; and an economic element, on the basis of a maritime GHG emissions pricing mechanism." *Source: IMO Press Briefing, IMO, 7 July 2023. https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/pressbriefings/pages/revised-ghg-reduction-strategy-for-global-shipping-adopted-.aspx

**UNECE/ITC (inland transport):** The ITC strategy is implemented through "61 United Nations legal instruments under ITC's purview" and draws on "avoid-shift-improve measures." *Source: UNECE Press Release, UNECE, 26 February 2024. https://unece.org/media/press/388574 The UNECE Executive Secretary noted: "Decarbonization of the transport sector is lagging behind many other sectors. This strategy will help countries to deliver on a significant part of their climate commitments under the Paris Agreement." *Source: UNECE Press Release, UNECE, 26 February 2024. https://unece.org/media/press/388574

**ITF/OECD (multimodal policy dialogue):** The ITF Decarbonising Transport initiative "currently unites more than 70 governments, organisations, institutions, foundations and companies" and has been designated by "the UN Climate Change Secretariat (UNFCCC) … as a focal point for transport under its Marrakech Partnership." *Source: ITF Decarbonising Transport initiative page, ITF/OECD, accessed 2026. https://www.itf-oecd.org/decarbonising-transport The initiative operates across "urban passenger transport, road freight transport, maritime transport, aviation and inter-urban transport." *Source: ITF Decarbonising Transport initiative page, ITF/OECD, accessed 2026. https://www.itf-oecd.org/decarbonising-transport

**ICCT (independent technical pressure):** The ICCT analysis finds the 2023 IMO revised strategy is "not compatible with 1.5°C but is compatible with well below 2°C," and that "international shipping will exceed its current share of the world's 1.5°C carbon budget by approximately 2032." *Source: ICCT Blog, ICCT, 7 July 2023. https://theicct.org/marine-imo-updated-ghg-strategy-jul23/ The ICCT is actively providing "technical advice and analysis to support policies that effectively reduce WTW CO₂e emissions from global shipping" and states it "will be in the room when the IMO revises its GHG strategy again in 2028." *Source: ICCT Blog, ICCT, 7 July 2023. https://theicct.org/marine-imo-updated-ghg-strategy-jul23/

*Analytical inference:* The ICCT's finding that the 1.5°C budget will be exceeded by approximately 2032 under the revised strategy creates sustained technical pressure on IMO member states to tighten the 2028 strategy revision — meaning the current checkpoints are more likely to be a floor than a ceiling for future regulatory ambition.

*Analytical inference:* The ITF's 70-partner coalition — spanning governments, IRU (road freight), Swedish Shipowners, FIA, and the European Commission — represents an unusually broad institutional base that accelerates translation of the decarbonization agenda from aspiration to instrument.

---

## 4. Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

**What moves this from policy signal to commercial pressure on the workspace:**

**IMO mid-term measures timeline (MEPC schedule, as published):**

The 2023 IMO GHG Strategy sets the following legislative schedule:

- MEPC 81 (Spring 2024): Interim report on Comprehensive impact assessment of the basket of candidate mid-term measures / Finalization of basket of measures
- MEPC 82 (Autumn 2024): Finalized report on Comprehensive impact assessment of the basket of candidate mid-term measures
- MEPC 83 (Spring 2025): Review of the short-term measure to be completed by 1 January 2026
- MEPC 84 (Spring 2026): Approval of measures / Review of the short-term measure (EEXI and CII) to be completed by 1 January 2026
- Extraordinary MEPC (Autumn 2025, six months after MEPC 83): Adoption of measures
- Target entry into force: 16 months after adoption, i.e. 2027
- MEPC 86 (Summer 2027): Initiate review of the 2023 IMO GHG Strategy
- MEPC 88 (Autumn 2028): Finalization of the review of the 2023 IMO GHG Strategy with a view to adoption of the 2028 IMO Strategy on reduction of GHG emissions from ships

*Source: IMO Press Briefing, IMO, 7 July 2023. https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/pressbriefings/pages/revised-ghg-reduction-strategy-for-global-shipping-adopted-.aspx

*Analytical inference:* The MEPC 84 (Spring 2026) approval window is the next major conversion event. If measures are approved on schedule, the entry-into-force window of approximately 2027 is when ocean carriers will face binding pricing or fuel-standard obligations that flow directly into freight rates and surcharges paid by forwarders and their clients.

**Existing short-term measures already in force:**

The ICCT confirms two short-term measures entered into force in 2023: the Energy Efficiency Existing Ship Index (EEXI) and the Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII). *Source: ICCT Blog, ICCT, 7 July 2023. https://theicct.org/marine-imo-updated-ghg-strategy-jul23/

*Analytical inference:* Both the EEXI and CII are "set to be revised by no later than January 1, 2026," per ICCT analysis of the IMO schedule. If revisions tighten EEXI and CII requirements, carrier operational costs rise in the 2026–2027 window — a direct pass-through risk for ocean freight rates.

**ITC inland transport trajectory:**

The ITC strategy sets a milestone of "developing a globally harmonized methodology to determine the carbon footprint of vehicles from cradle-to-grave by 2025." The strategy is subject to "a five-yearly review, with the first review due in 2029." *Source: UNECE Press Release, UNECE, 26 February 2024. https://unece.org/media/press/388574

*Analytical inference:* The 2025 cradle-to-grave carbon footprint methodology, if adopted, would provide the technical basis for road carrier Scope 3 reporting obligations that the workspace's clients increasingly require under CSRD and ISSB frameworks — converting what is currently a voluntary data-collection challenge into a standardised upstream demand.

**EU parallel track (regional acceleration):**

The ICCT notes that the European Union has acted "under FuelEU Maritime, which is similar to the proposed IMO GFS, and by incorporating shipping into its Emissions Trading System." *Source: ICCT Blog, ICCT, 7 July 2023. https://theicct.org/marine-imo-updated-ghg-strategy-jul23/ This regional instrument is already operative and creates cost differentials on European port calls regardless of IMO timeline.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* The precise scope of EU ETS applicability to freight forwarders acting as non-vessel operating common carriers (NVOCCs) or as agents for the shipper of record — versus the verified gross tonnage operator bearing the obligation directly — requires counsel review of EU ETS Directive provisions and 2023 amendments extending scope to shipping.

---

## 5. Operational and Cost Implications If It Materialises

**COST ALERT**

**Ocean mode (tertiary, but highest unit-cost exposure per container):**

The IMO's "basket of measures" is explicitly designed as both a technical element (fuel standard) and an economic element (emissions pricing mechanism). *Source: IMO Press Briefing, IMO, 7 July 2023. https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/pressbriefings/pages/revised-ghg-reduction-strategy-for-global-shipping-adopted-.aspx An emissions pricing mechanism, once adopted, converts into a per-tonne-of-cargo surcharge passed through ocean carrier tariffs.

*Analytical inference:* The ICCT's estimate that aligning with 1.5°C would require "a 38% reduction in the WTW GHG intensity of marine fuels by 2030, 97% by 2040, and 100% by 2050" illustrates the pace of fuel transition required. *Source: ICCT Blog, ICCT, 7 July 2023. https://theicct.org/marine-imo-updated-ghg-strategy-jul23/ Zero and near-zero fuels currently carry significant cost premiums over conventional bunker fuel; the workspace's ocean freight cost baseline will rise as carriers absorb these costs.

*Specific cost uplift figure per TEU not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-12.*

*Analytical inference:* For cargo verticals where the workspace ships high-value, time-sensitive freight (fine art, live events equipment, high-value automotive) by ocean on low-frequency lanes, the cost uplift per unit may be manageable in absolute terms but will require new line-item visibility in client quotations — particularly if the EU ETS surcharge for shipping is already being levied on European port-call legs.

**Road mode (secondary, broadest geographic exposure):**

Transport accounts for "some 23% of annual GHG emissions. Inland transport accounts for 72% of this total, with 69% coming from road transport." *Source: UNECE Press Release, UNECE, 26 February 2024. https://unece.org/media/press/388574 The ITC strategy's emphasis on "accelerated substitution of fossil-fuelled vehicles with zero emission vehicles" and "replacing parts and retrofitting" creates capex pressure on road hauliers that will flow into drayage and linehaul rates.

*Analytical inference:* Road freight demand is "projected to increase by 100% by 2050" per the UNECE source, while the sector simultaneously faces mandatory decarbonization. The combination — rising volumes, rising compliance costs — is structurally inflationary for road rates on a 5–10 year horizon.

*Specific rate uplift for drayage or linehaul not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-12.*

**Air mode (primary for the workspace):**

The ITF Decarbonising Transport initiative covers aviation as one of its "in-depth sectoral studies" modes. *Source: ITF Decarbonising Transport initiative page, ITF/OECD, accessed 2026. https://www.itf-oecd.org/decarbonising-transport UNCTAD and UNECE source blocks do not provide aviation-specific cost or regulatory data; the air mode cost implications are governed by CORSIA and EU ETS aviation provisions, which are outside the scope of these source blocks.

*Specific cost figures not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-12 — see IMO/ICAO source coverage for aviation.*

**Developing-country trade lanes (Americas ex-US, Africa, Asia-Pacific):**

The IMO strategy explicitly notes: "Particular attention should be paid to the needs of developing countries, especially SIDS and LDCs." *Source: IMO Press Briefing, IMO, 7 July 2023. https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/pressbriefings/pages/revised-ghg-reduction-strategy-for-global-shipping-adopted-.aspx UNCTAD's own programmes — "Climate-smart trade facilitation in Africa and Asia-Pacific," "Sustainable and Resilient Food Supply Chains" — signal that the workspace's trade lanes into these regions will intersect with capacity-building interventions that may alter port performance and logistics reliability on those corridors. *Source: UNCTAD Transport, Logistics and Trade Facilitation topic page, UNCTAD, accessed 2026. https://unctad.org/topic/transport-and-trade-logistics

*Analytical inference:* Freight forwarders moving cargo through ports in Sub-Saharan Africa and Pacific island states should expect that UNCTAD's sustainable smart ports programme will gradually raise port environmental compliance standards — potentially affecting port call times and handling costs as infrastructure is upgraded.

---

## 6. Competitive Implications

**COMPETITIVE EDGE**

*No named competitor positioning is sourced from the provided blocks. The following operates under the competitive lens using publicly grounded structural facts.*

**Who has early access:** Carriers and forwarders who begin structured carbon accounting for ocean freight now — before IMO mid-term measures enter force in approximately 2027 — will have auditable emissions data available when clients formally require it under CSRD Scope 3 reporting or RFP scoring criteria. This is a data-infrastructure lead, not a regulatory compliance lead.

*Analytical inference:* Forwarders who can offer clients a modality-specific emissions footprint for a shipment — distinguishing ocean leg, road drayage leg, and air leg — will be positioned to respond to enterprise client procurement requirements that are already appearing in European logistics tenders. The ITF initiative's role as "a conduit for the exchange of information between the transport sector and the UNFCCC" means that client sustainability teams are increasingly aware of the policy architecture; forwarders who are not will be outmatched in conversations with well-briefed procurement officers.

*Analytical inference:* The ITC strategy's development of "a globally harmonized methodology to determine the carbon footprint of vehicles from cradle-to-grave by 2025" — once published — will create a standardised reporting basis that large forwarders with early data systems adopt faster than smaller competitors.

**UNCTAD Global Supply Chain Forum 2026** is scheduled for 29 November – 1 December 2026 in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia. *Source: UNCTAD Transport, Logistics and Trade Facilitation topic page, UNCTAD, accessed 2026. https://unctad.org/topic/transport-and-trade-logistics This is a convening event at which sustainable supply chain standards and freight logistics policy will be on the agenda — and where client organisations, port authorities, and regulators will form positions that cascade into commercial expectations.

---

## 7. Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

1. "The IMO adopted a revised net-zero-by-2050 strategy for international shipping in July 2023, with binding checkpoints at 2030 and 2040. The regulatory instruments to implement it — fuel standards and an emissions pricing mechanism — are expected to enter force around 2027. We are tracking the MEPC 84 session in Spring 2026 as the approval decision point." *(Grounded: IMO Press Briefing, 7 July 2023)*

2. "The UN also adopted a parallel strategy for road, rail, and inland waterway decarbonization in February 2024. That strategy is working toward a globally harmonized vehicle carbon footprint methodology by 2025, which will eventually underpin the Scope 3 data your sustainability team will need from us." *(Grounded: UNECE Press Release, 26 February 2024)*

3. "We can break down the emissions profile of your shipment by mode. The ocean leg sits under IMO/EU ETS governance; the road leg sits under evolving national implementations of the ITC framework. The air leg has its own CORSIA framework." *(Analytical inference — appropriate to present as internal analytical work, not regulatory authority)*

**What to avoid claiming:**

- Do not claim the workspace is "compliant" with the IMO GHG Strategy — the strategy's headline target applies to carriers, not forwarders, and the mid-term binding instruments are not yet in force.
- Do not quote a specific dollar-per-tonne carbon price for ocean freight — no sourced figure exists as of this brief.
- Do not cite the ICCT analysis as regulatory authority. It is independent analysis (Tier 6), not IMO official guidance.

**Questions to pose to clients:**

- "Has your sustainability team begun mapping Scope 3 Category 4 (upstream transportation) emissions? We can help structure the data collection now, before disclosure frameworks make it mandatory."
- "Does your ocean carrier contract include a GHG surcharge clause? If not, what is your exposure when the IMO pricing mechanism enters force around 2027?"

---

## 8. What the Workspace Should Do Now

**MONITORING — re-check window: MEPC 84 outcome (Spring 2026) and MEPC Extraordinary session (Autumn 2025)**

**Action 1 — Track MEPC 84 outcome (Spring 2026).**
MEPC 84 is the scheduled approval session for the IMO basket of mid-term measures. The outcome determines whether the maritime emissions pricing mechanism enters force around 2027 and at what intensity. Assign a named operations or compliance contact to monitor IMO press briefings following MEPC 84.
*Why now:* The MEPC schedule published in the 2023 strategy makes this the most proximate legislative decision point. *Source: IMO Press Briefing, IMO, 7 July 2023.*

**Action 2 — Begin modal emissions data collection for ocean freight legs.**
Before the IMO pricing mechanism enters force, the workspace should establish a baseline emissions-per-tonne-kilometre record by carrier, vessel class, and route. This enables: (a) client reporting under CSRD Scope 3; (b) internal cost modelling when surcharges arrive; (c) carrier selection on GHG efficiency grounds.
*Operational gap:* If the workspace currently manages ocean freight bookings without capturing vessel-level CII ratings or GHG intensity data, this is a foundational gap to close in 2026.
*Cost of inaction:* When enterprise clients formally require Scope 3 Category 4 data (which CSRD mandates for large EU-listed companies reporting from FY2024–2025), forwarders without auditable emissions records will lose accounts to competitors who have them.

**Action 3 — Add a GHG surcharge review clause to ocean carrier contracts at next renewal.**
*Legal Confirmation Required:* The specific contract language needed to protect the workspace's pass-through rights when the IMO pricing mechanism enters force — and whether existing force majeure or fuel adjustment clauses cover a new emissions levy — requires counsel review.

**Action 4 — Register for UNCTAD Global Supply Chain Forum 2026 (Riyadh, 29 Nov – 1 Dec 2026).**
This event is the most proximate senior-level convening where sustainable supply chain standards affecting the workspace's freight operations will be debated. Direct engagement provides advance intelligence on the directions UNCTAD technical assistance programmes are moving — which shapes port standards, trade facilitation requirements, and logistics policy in developing markets the workspace serves.
*Source: UNCTAD Transport, Logistics and Trade Facilitation topic page, UNCTAD, accessed 2026. https://unctad.org/topic/transport-and-trade-logistics

**Action 5 — Monitor UNECE ITC vehicle carbon footprint methodology (target: 2025).**
The ITC Climate Action Plan includes "developing a globally harmonized methodology to determine the carbon footprint of vehicles from cradle-to-grave by 2025." *Source: UNECE Press Release, UNECE, 26 February 2024.* If this methodology is published, the workspace's road carrier data collection requirements will be directly shaped by it. Check UNECE ITC publications quarterly.

---
